author: @peterbreis5407
text:
What about using compressed air from a blowhole chamber powered by the rising and falling of the waves, using a flipping butterfly valve to keep the air moving in one direction through a turbine. 

The air will smooth out the energy and you can capture the wave off-shore and feed the compressed air to a land-based turbine via a pneumatic hose. That way you keep the crucial parts out of the harsh ocean environment.

By staggering the blowhole chambers across the wave action you can further amplify and smooth out the energy collected.

author: @peterengland4745
text: Hi Rob.  You are right!  Waves are huge potential sources of energy.  You can, however, get rotary power directly from waves without any mechanical mechanisms.  As shown in your diagram of waves, there is a circular motion in the water beneath the wave.  It is a flow of water that rotates through 360 degrees as each wave passes.  If you put an eccentric vane on a stationary shaft in the water under the wave it will rotate once in the flow of each wave.  This then produces rotary motion directly from the kinetic energy (the flow of water) of the wave.  The amount of energy the vane can harvest will governed by the height of the wave (trough to crest), by the depth of the vane in the water beneath the wave, and by the size of the vane.

author: @nigelwilliams7920
text:
Wonderful work as always!  

A feature of (in this case) wave energy, but shared by all sorts of energy converters from natural sources, is the intermittency of the input compared with the nice even output required for domestic use.

With your tidal arm, to attenuate the output you could use the arm to pump water up into a top tank, and then run a fixed speed turbine and alternator on the discharge from the tank into the bottom tank to give you 240 V 50 Hz (or what ever you require).

I find that step between intermittent and widely variable input and the required steady output is the biggest hurdle for any comprehensive natural energy capture system.  

I like the thought experiment where you have say a huge flywheel rotating, and you want to add the energy from a falling feather to the energy in the huge flywheel.  Its a problem worth solving.

Conceptually, use the input to generate some electrical current in a wire.  Boost the voltage and frequency of that way up  - say 4000 Volts and 1000 Hz, regardless of the current  The high frequency enables very effecient AC to DC conversion, and then a PWM setup provides the 50Hz and a simple 4000 V / 240 V transformer gets the required voltage.

The trick is to get ALL your input devices to sing the same 4000 V 1000 Hz tune (i.e. to fill the 'top tank'), regardless of the current they are providing.

Its a dark and stormy night here with torrential rain and a decent gale.  (Its my fault, I just upgraded my wind vane - hence the blow!)  The down pipes from my roof could give me about 60 Watts of energy as the rain falls through the available three metres,  while the gale could provide a swag more Watts with a suitable converter.  These two intermittent and unmatched inputs have to somehow get aligned to 'fill the top tank' which can then be drained by the output system at the required 240 V 50 Hz.

In this day and age any fool can make sparks come out of a sun or wind or water driven device.  The trick is to get that largesse into a suitable form for the end user to do things with when its needed.

Your universal solution to this intractable problem is eagerly anticipated by your adoring congregation!!!  :)

author: @ClownWhisper
text:
I've always wanted to make something that would just capture the water from the wave and or the tide but mainly the wave you could make something like a chamber with a trough in front of it the waves would go up the ramp and just fill the container and keep filling it while it also released it underneath and in front of so you could put a paddle wheel or some kind of turbine to catch that exiting water. It would always be filling and always be draining the only problem is is positioning it in such a spot as to not be overwhelmed by the tides perhaps positioning it and some stainless rods that would allow it to float up and down with the tides so it basically remains roughly in the same position relative to the water level period now I understand that this would not be easy it would take a lot of trial and error and if I lived anywhere even remotely close to a body of water that I could test this I would.

It would allow for river type collection oh energy without the need to find a river somewhere your river would be the trough ramp leading up to the chamber that is to be filled you could actually have the paddle wheel sticking out of the trough to be turned in One Direction while the water exits to the bottom of the paddle wheel helping it along in the same direction maybe I'll draw it and send it to you Robert

author: @af0ulwind
text:
perhaps this is a new concept maybe not.. but....

a hollow sphere, well several hollow spheres with a large funnel with the spout going up through the sphere and out the top. a catch basin around the spout and a down sloped flue that swivels around the catch basin to direct the water's return to the body of water....
bind the spheres together with all the spouts aiming up, build your platform of house boat or ship around the spheres mounting to them at the largest circumference.. and above... Use the flues to change the vessels directional movement. use turbines on the flues to produce power.

the weight of the vessel will resist the swell of the wave forcing the water to accelerate up the funnel and out over the catch basins... with a one way valve in the up spout you can use this to pump water to your salination facility or filter or anything else...

author: @hubrisnaut
text: It's an interesting design but seems more complicated than it has to be (which means more maintenance and a higher probability of material failure). Wave powered generators can be made with a coil, magnets and a housing. A simple one can be made with a water tight tube with a coil and a magnet that slides inside the tube as it rides on the waves (there are videos here on YT). Another one uses a tethered housing and a direct buoyancy driven magnet to move it past the coil. There are a few ways to do it.

author: @Xero1of1
text:
This is cool. As far as wave power goes, though, there's a version that I personally prefer. It's not mobile by any means, and it would be a little difficult to do, but, what you do is pour a gigantic cement box. Either the top or shore end of the box has a small 12" circular hole, and the sea end of the box has a long horizontal opening across its length. This box would be 20-30' long at least and probably the same width. The side with the horizontal opening must be placed so the opening is completely submerged, even in the troughs of the waves at low tide. The box must be tall enough to have a few feet of air space at high tide. The idea here is that as the waves come in, they constantly force air into and out of the box. The bigger the waves, the more air is moved. The 12" circular hole is the only outlet for the air, so you put an air turbine in it that is unidirectional, meaning whether the air goes in or comes out, it only spins in one direction. Since it's just a big concrete box, it's easy to make. Since the turbine would be on the shore side or on top of the box, servicing it would be very easy. The bigger the volume of air inside the box, the more pressure is built up and thus the higher the speed of the air passing through the turbine. 

If I had ocean-front property and was allowed to build a pier into the water from concrete, I would turn that pier into a wave/air generator and use that to power my house. The waves will always wave since the wind will always blow, so I'll always be generating. 24/7, day and night, summer, winter, nuclear annihilation, what have you. So long as the horizontal opening on the sea side remains below the water and the 12" circular hole remains above water, it'll generate.

replies.author: @AndreaDingbatt
replies.text:
That sounds great!!
But, perhaps if you were to use the force of the water to capture the energy?!
Just a thought, because the water is  able to provide more torque...
Namasté 🙏💞
Andréa and Critters.   ...XxX....

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i have seen designs like that mate - the Australian blowhole generator isn't far off from what you are suggesting

replies.author: @Xero1of1
replies.text: @AndreaDingbatt You're correct, in that the water would provide more torque. It is a lot heavier than air after all. There's an issue though. You're not going to get much speed out of it. What you'd have to do is force a large volume of water through a very small area to create the effect of water pressure that a normal hydroelectric dam would have. Air, being lighter, can move a lot faster and can be compressed much easier than water. Additionally, in order to capture the power from the water, you'd have to have a collector IN the water... that requires a lot more maintenance. Seaweed, critters, any number of things could jam it up. With just an opening for the water to go into and out of, the odds that it gets jammed are very low. So you end up with a large volume of water moving into and out of the concrete cube rather easily which can create a strong air pressure or vacuum in the airspace within the cube, and by restricting the area that the air can enter into and leave out of to attempt to equalize, you can increase the speed of the air moving through it.
